text.title: Decision-Options Memo Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that prevent the two recurring failures: a memo that is actually a one-option recommendation, and a memo that hides the dissent.

rule.statement: The memo MUST present at least three viable options. If only two come to mind, "do nothing" or "defer" is included as the third. More than five is rejected — the BA filters first. Single-option memos are sent back for re-framing.

rationale.source: Decision-analysis practice; cf. Kahneman, Thinking Fast and Slow on framing effects

rule.rationale: Two-option memos default to false dichotomy; single-option memos default to rubber-stamp approval. Three forces real trade-off articulation.

rule.statement: Each option MUST have a Cost-Value-Risk row populated: Cost (effort, $$$, time), Value (business outcome enabled, in measurable units where possible), Risk (top 1-3 risks with likelihood and impact). All three are populated even if a value is "negligible" or "none identified" — empty cells are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without parallel structure across options, the decision-maker compares apples to oranges. Forcing the same shape per option produces apples-to-apples comparison.

rule.statement: The recommendation section MUST name one option and explicitly state "Why not Option B" and "Why not Option C" in 1-2 sentences each. Recommendations without why-not text are rejected — they hide the trade.

rationale.source: Amazon 6-page memo discipline + good staff-work practice

rule.rationale: Why-not language forces the BA to engage with the other options seriously. Without it, the memo silently treats the recommendation as obvious, which it rarely is.

rule.statement: The dissent section MUST name dissenting stakeholders (by name, not "the team") with their objection summarized in 1-2 sentences each. If no dissent surfaces in consultation, the BA writes "no dissenting view captured from [list of stakeholders consulted]." Generic "some concerns were raised" is rejected.
rule.rationale: Anonymous dissent allows the decision-maker to dismiss it; named dissent forces engagement. Empty dissent sections with disagreement under the surface produce decisions that unravel post-meeting.

rule.statement: Every memo MUST state: decision-required-by (ISO date), decision-maker (named individual), default-if-no-decision (one of the options auto-selected, or "project blocked"). Open-ended memos rot in queues.

rationale.source: Project-management practice; cf. Andy Grove, High Output Management on decision timeliness

rule.rationale: A decision without a deadline is a wish. The default-if-no-decision clause is the forcing function — it tells the decision-maker what happens if they ignore the memo.

example.title: Compliant Cost-Value-Risk row
example.code: Option B: Use AWS RDS Aurora for the data layer Cost: $2,400/mo at expected load; ~3 weeks setup + DBA training. Value: Native multi-AZ HA without app code; backup/restore automated; SOC2-relevant audit logs out of box. Risk: Vendor lock-in (M/H, low likelihood near-term); cost scaling unpredictable above 10 vCPU (M/M); migration off Postgres OSS is non-trivial (H/L).
example.note: Concrete numbers, value tied to outcomes, risks have L/I and severity. Reader can compare to Option A/C in 30 seconds.

example.title: Empty structure
example.code: Option B: AWS RDS Cost: A bit more Value: Better reliability Risk: Some risks
example.note: Decorative table. Doesn't enable any comparison. Violates r2.
